text: The surgeon performs a diagnostic laparoscopy to reduce the contents of a lower abdominal incisional hernia, using laparoscopic instruments visible on the screen. A monopolar current and blunt grasper are used to manipulate the hernia sac. The surgeon sizes the hernia defect with a scale to select an appropriate mesh, which is placed at the start of the procedure. The robotic Da Vinci XI system is then docked to begin dissection.

We go in with a plan of doing an extraperitoneal repair. Now this repair can either be tap repair or a tongue repair. I wouldn't usually commit to it before I have already started the surgery and I see the planes of dissection. What my go-to approach is that in cases of incisional hernias, I do like to do a term or a repair where the interface separating the mesh and the abdominal content would be lot of how do I put it scar tissue rather than the true peritoneum itself and it tends to have multiple buttonholes and it really doesn't have much of strength so I would always be more comfortable with a stronger layer that I can dissect that I can handle and it will separate the implant from the abdominal Also the important part would be that you'll be able to suture this this layer well together. A flimsy peritoneum would just be a recipe for disaster when it comes to suturing it back and doing a closure of such case.

The surgeon employs a transabdominal retromuscular approach, dividing the hernia sac and encountering flimsy peritoneum with buttonholes. The sac is completely divided, revealing a hole where it was connected. The dissection creates the space of Retzius in the midline and the space of Bogros laterally. Careful dissection is performed around the inferior epigastric vessels, with attention to the glomerular bladder and retro pubic veins as the dissection progresses inferiorly.

The surgeon performs a gentle dissection, following the loose areolar tissue to maintain the correct plane. Small vessels encountered are cauterized to achieve hemostasis. A scale is used to measure the defect, which is approximately four by five centimeters. A tailored mesh, measuring thirteen by fifteen centimeters, is placed to ensure adequate overlap and coverage. The dissection is completed to about eighty to ninety percent before placing the mesh, with additional dissection performed as needed to create a precise space for the mesh.

The surgeon creates a well-defined space for the mesh placement, ensuring it sits comfortably without creases. Hemostasis is achieved, and the surgeon navigates to the correct plane on the right side, preserving the inferior epigastric artery. Small blood vessels are divided, and a lump of preperitoneal fat is encountered, indicating a herniation through a small defect.

The surgeon navigates from the space of Phrygius to the space of Bogros, creating an adequate space for mesh placement without excessive dissection. Hemostasis is achieved, and the mesh is positioned securely. The surgeon uses a barbed suture to close the hernia defect, employing a backhand suturing technique to ensure stability and prevent tissue slippage. The retropubic area is refined to prevent mesh tenting or folding, with clear anatomical landmarks visible.

The surgeon closes the hernia defect using a backhand stitch technique, ensuring a snug and non-strangulating closure. Absorbable sutures are used, allowing scar tissue formation to reinforce the mesh placement. Orientation stitches are placed with 3-0 Vicryl to align the mesh symmetrically along the midline, and the mesh is fixed behind the pubic arch.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection to create space for the mesh, ensuring it is securely positioned without excessive movement. A 3-0 Stratafix barbed suture is used to close the PRS Peritoneum Complex, with multiple consecutive bites taken to achieve proper alignment and an aesthetically pleasing closure. The surgeon crosses the midline and continues suturing on the opposite side, addressing small buttonholes and herniated defects during the peritoneal closure.

The surgeon closes the main incision using sutures before addressing smaller defects. Defects larger than five to eight millimeters are closed with figure-of-eight or simple sutures to prevent bowel or omentum from entering and causing complications.
